target.name: annotinolide B
target.motivation: Annotinolide B contains a challenging small-ring cyclobutane embedded within its intricate framework and a strained 7-membered lactone, along with three quaternary stereocenters, making it an interesting synthetic target. It is also the first Lycopodium alkaloid to inhibit the aggregation of the amyloid protein Aβ1-42.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: gold-catalyzed Conia-ene reaction to furnish the bicyclic core
intermolecular allene [2+2] photocycloaddition to obtain a single cyclobutane adduct

success_criteria.free_text: First total synthesis of annotinolide B.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-O bond of the lactone and C-C bond of the cyclobutane
key_disconnections.reasoning: To concurrently assemble the cyclobutane and strained lactone present in 14 from tricycle 15.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-N bond of the N-acyl pyridone
key_disconnections.reasoning: N-acyl dihydropyridone 15 would be available via partial reduction of the corresponding N-acyl pyridone, itself obtained via an oxidative cyclization of pyridone alcohol 16.

N-H pyridone 16 was envisioned to arise from reduction of a lactone, which can be constructed stereoselectively from the dihydroquinoline methyl ester 17 through an iodolactonization/deiodination sequence.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond of the benzenoid ring
key_disconnections.reasoning: Dihydroquinoline 17, whose benzenoid ring is dearomatized, could be synthesized from functionalized quinoline 18 through photochemical semireduction chemistry.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: Our retrosynthetic design represents a novel entry into Lycopodium alkaloid chemical space, where a simple quinoline is dearomatized sequentially (benzenoid ring then pyridine ring) to build complexity. The strategy involves late-stage functional group interconversions on cyclobutane 14, an advanced intermediate bearing the full framework of 3. The key steps include an intramolecular [2+2] photocycloaddition, oxidative cyclization, iodolactonization/deiodination, and photochemical benzenoid dearomatization.

thought.background: Commercial hydroxyquinoline 19 is a readily available starting material.
thought.gap: Need to convert hydroxyquinoline 19 to bromoquinoline 20.
thought.decision: To prepare bromoquinoline 20, chose benzylation of 19 with benzyl bromide and potassium carbonate, expecting a high yield.

outcome.observation: Known bromoquinoline 20 was obtained in 99% yield from commercial hydroxyquinoline 19.

evidence.text_span: In the forward direction, known bromoquinoline 2014 (one step from commercial hydroxyquinoline 19) was engaged in a Heck reaction with methyl acrylate using XPhos Pd G2, delivering 21 in 76% yield on >13 g scale (Scheme 1).

thought.gap: Need to introduce the acrylate moiety via a Heck reaction.
thought.decision: To introduce the acrylate, chose a Heck reaction with methyl acrylate using XPhos Pd G2, expecting good yield and regioselectivity.

outcome.observation: Heck reaction delivered 21 in 76% yield on >13 g scale.

evidence.text_span: In the forward direction, known bromoquinoline 2014 (one step from commercial hydroxyquinoline 19) was engaged in a Heck reaction with methyl acrylate using XPhos Pd G2, delivering 21 in 76% yield on >13 g scale (Scheme 1).

thought.background: Quinoline 21 contains an alkene and a benzyl ether.
thought.gap: Need to chemoselectively reduce the alkene without affecting the benzyl ether.
thought.decision: To chemoselectively reduce the alkene, chose NaBH4 with CuSO4 and CoCl2 as cocatalysts, expecting smooth reduction.

outcome.observation: Chemoselective reduction of the alkene proceeded smoothly, affording 18 in excellent yield (87%) on decagram scale.

evidence.text_span: A chemoselective reduction of the alkene in the presence of the benzyl ether proceeded smoothly with NaBH4 and inexpensive CuSO4 and CoCl2 as cocatalysts, affording 18 in excellent yield (87%) on decagram scale.16

thought.gap: Need to perform the key photochemical dearomatization of the benzenoid ring to 17.
thought.decision: To achieve photochemical dearomatization, chose modified conditions reported by the Ma group using TFA and Ir[dF(CF3)ppy]2(dtbbpy)PF6 (22), expecting the desired dihydroquinoline 17.

outcome.observation: The desired dihydroquinoline 17 was obtained, contaminated with a small amount of the fully saturated compound 24, in a combined yield of 52% (43% of 17). The reaction was readily scalable (8.7 g of 18).

evidence.text_span: With substituted quinoline 18 in hand, we were set to explore the key photochemical dearomatization to 17. We ultimately found success by slightly modifying conditions reported by the Ma group using less expensive TFA and Ir[dF(CF3)ppy]2(dtbbpy)PF6 (22) (instead of Gd(OTf)3 and Ir[dF(Me)ppy]2(dtbbpy)PF6, respectively).12b These conditions led to the desired dihydroquinoline 17 contaminated with a small amount of the fully saturated compound 24 in a combined yield of 52% (43% of 17), with the reaction proving readily scalable (8.7 g of 18).

thought.background: A mixture of 17 and 24 is available. Only 17 reacts in the iodolactonization.
thought.gap: Need to perform ester hydrolysis and iodolactonization to set the syn-C-5 oxygen stereocenter, followed by deiodination.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose a one-pot ester hydrolysis/iodolactonization sequence with K2CO3 and NIS, followed by radical deiodination with (TMS)3SiH and AIBN, expecting lactone 25.

outcome.observation: Lactone 25 was obtained in 74% yield over 2 steps (based on 17). This enabled the key syn-C-5 oxygen stereocenter to be set.

evidence.text_span: The mixture of 17 and 24 was taken forward into a one-pot ester hydrolysis/iodolactonization sequence17 where only 17 reacts in the latter reaction. This enables the key syn-C-5 oxygen stereocenter to be set in the intermediate iodolactone, which was then deiodinated under radical conditions to obtain lactone 25 in 74% yield over 2 steps (based on 17).

thought.gap: Need to reduce the lactone to a diol, protect it, and then selectively deprotect the primary alcohol to obtain 26.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose reduction with LiAlH4, followed by double TES-protection with TESOTf, and then selective removal of the primary TES ether with PPTS in EtOH, expecting 26.

outcome.observation: This 3-step sequence from 25 to 26 proceeded in excellent overall yield (95%).

evidence.text_span: Lactone 25 was reduced with LiAlH4 to deliver a diol, which could be converted to its secondary triethylsilyl (TES) ether 26 through double TES-protection, followed by selective removal of the primary TES ether with PPTS in EtOH.18 This 3-step sequence from 25 to 26 proceeded in excellent overall yield (95%).

thought.gap: Need to remove the O-benzyl group and then perform an oxidative cyclization to form the N-acyl pyridone 27.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose standard Pd/C hydrogenation for benzyl removal, followed by oxidative cyclization with TEMPO and PhI(OAc)2, expecting 27.

outcome.observation: The O-benzyl group was removed, and the resultant crude N-H pyridone 16 was engaged in an oxidative cyclization to deliver the desired tricyclic N-acyl pyridone 27 in 79% yield (2 steps).

evidence.text_span: Next, the O-benzyl group was removed via standard Pd/C hydrogenation, and the resultant crude N-H pyridone 16 was engaged in an oxidative cyclization through its free primary alcohol with TEMPO and PhI(OAc)219 to deliver the desired tricyclic N-acyl pyridone 27 in 79% yield (2 steps).

thought.gap: Need to reduce 27 to dihydropyridone 28 by saturating the less hindered disubstituted double bond.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose heterogeneous hydrogenation with Pd/C as catalyst, expecting consistent product formation while carefully monitoring to avoid overreduction.

outcome.observation: Heterogeneous hydrogenation with Pd/C as catalyst emerged as the only condition that led to consistent product formation (70%), though careful monitoring is required to avoid overreduction byproducts 29 and 30, and the reaction appeared sensitive to the solvent identity and its water content.

evidence.text_span: The planned reduction of pyridone 27 to dihydropyridone 28 via saturation of the less hindered disubstituted double bond turned out to be nontrivial (see SI for screens). Ultimately, heterogeneous hydrogenation with Pd/C as catalyst emerged as the only condition that led to consistent product formation (70%), though careful monitoring is required to avoid overreduction byproducts20 29 and 30, and the reaction appeared sensitive to the solvent identity and its water content (see SI for details).

thought.gap: Need to reveal the secondary alcohol by TES-deprotection.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose HF·pyridine for TES-deprotection, expecting a high yield.

outcome.observation: The secondary alcohol was revealed via TES-deprotection using HF·pyridine (95%).

evidence.text_span: With reliable access to N-acyl dihydropyridone 28, its secondary alcohol could be revealed via TES-deprotection using HF·pyridine (95%).

thought.gap: Need to prepare the [2+2]-precursor 15 via esterification with methacrylic acid.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose esterification with methacrylic acid under Yamaguchi conditions, expecting a smooth reaction.

outcome.observation: The desired [2+2]-precursor 15 was obtained smoothly (80%) via esterification with methacrylic acid under Yamaguchi conditions.

evidence.text_span: The desired [2+2]-precursor 15 was then obtained smoothly (80%) via esterification with methacrylic acid under Yamaguchi conditions.21

thought.gap: Need to perform the key intramolecular [2+2] photocycloaddition to form cyclobutane lactone 14.
thought.decision: To achieve this, screened many conditions including different irradiation wavelengths, solvents, additives, and sensitizers, expecting the desired cyclobutane lactone 14.

outcome.observation: The desired cyclobutane lactone 14 was never obtained. Only undesired aromatized byproducts such as 32 were isolated in variable yields.

evidence.text_span: With substrate 15 in hand, we were set to test our key [2 + 2] photocycloaddition reaction. Unfortunately, while many conditions were screened, including different irradiation wavelengths, solvents, additives, and sensitizers (see SI for full details), the desired cyclobutane lactone 14 was never obtained. Only undesired aromatized byproducts such as 32 were isolated in variable yields.

thought.background: Intramolecular [2+2] photocycloaddition of 15 failed, possibly due to strain in forming a 4/7-ring system concurrently.
thought.gap: Need to explore an intermolecular [2+2] cycloaddition to form the cyclobutane first, then the 7-membered lactone.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose to irradiate 31 in the presence of excess methyl methacrylate, expecting a cyclobutane product.

outcome.observation: Preliminary experiments delivered a single cyclobutane product 34 in 40% yield. However, 34 possessed incorrect connectivity (undesired head-to-tail regioselectivity) and occurred from the undesired α-face (confirmed by X-ray crystallographic analysis).

evidence.text_span: Most directly, the cyclobutane would arise through an intermolecular [2 + 2] cycloaddition with methyl methacrylate.23 Encouragingly, preliminary experiments where 31 was irradiated at 365 nm in the presence of excess methyl methacrylate delivered a single cyclobutane product 34 in 40% yield. Unfortunately, further analysis showed 34 to possess the incorrect connectivity for 3 (determined by 2D NMR analysis), resulting from undesired head-to-tail regioselectivity. Moreover, the cycloaddition occurred from the undesired α- face (confirmed by X-ray crystallographic analysis).

thought.background: The initial intramolecular [2+2] photocycloaddition failed, possibly due to strain in forming a 4/7-ring system concurrently and lack of flexibility in the ester tether. Intermolecular [2+2] also failed due to regioselectivity and stereoselectivity issues.
thought.gap: Need to revisit the intramolecular cycloaddition strategy with different tethers that offer greater flexibility and form a more favorable 4/6-ring system.
thought.decision: To overcome this, decided to explore β-silyl acrylates (e.g., 35) as they offer greater flexibility and form a more favorable six-membered ring alongside the cyclobutane ring (4/6-ring system) in 36, with the silyl tether being removable via protodesilylation. This requires the synthesis of a (Z)-β-silyl acrylate 38.

evidence.text_span: We reasoned that forming both the cyclobutane and the 7- membered lactone (4/7-ring system) concurrently may be a highly strained process, with the lack of flexibility in the ester tether likely aggravating this issue.22 To overcome this challenge, we decided to explore the initial construction of the cyclobutane followed by the 7-membered lactone in a subsequent step. Most directly, the cyclobutane would arise through an intermolecular [2 + 2] cycloaddition with methyl methacrylate.23 Encouragingly, preliminary experiments where 31 was irradiated at 365 nm in the presence of excess methyl methacrylate delivered a single cyclobutane product 34 in 40% yield. Unfortunately, further analysis showed 34 to possess the incorrect connectivity for 3 (determined by 2D NMR analysis), resulting from undesired head-to-tail regioselectivity. Moreover, the cycloaddition occurred from the undesired α- face (confirmed by X-ray crystallographic analysis). This latter fact was somewhat surprising given the similarities between our scaffold and Wiesner's (10),7 which underwent [2 + 2] cycloaddition selectively on the β-face, though 31 contains an adjacent axial β-OH group potentially blocking the desired approach.23,24 Since an intermolecular [2 + 2] cycloaddition had proven problematic, we decided to revisit the intramolecular cycloaddition strategy with different tethers (Scheme 2). Among the many possibilities (see SI for other tethers tested), β-silyl acrylates25 (e.g., 35) appeared the most appealing. In this case, a more favorable six-membered ring would form alongside the cyclobutane ring (4/6-ring system) in 36, with the silyl tether offering greater flexibility than the previous ester linkage, while also being removable via protodesilylation. Of potential concern, however, was the absence of literature reports on the viability of β-silyl acrylates in [2 + 2] cycloadditions.26 According to the above plan, a (Z)-β-silyl acrylate (38) is required, as the (Z)-geometry of the alkene would translate to the desired C-15 epimer at the ester-bearing quaternary carbon of the cyclobutane following [2 + 2] cycloaddition (assuming no competitive alkene photoisomerization). No general method to synthesize trisubstituted (Z)-β-silyl acrylates of the type 38 is known in the literature. We thus developed a 5- step synthesis from the known (Z)-vinyl iodide 3727 3727 (see SI for synthetic details).

thought.background: A (Z)-β-silyl acrylate 38 is required for the revised intramolecular [2+2] cycloaddition strategy.
thought.gap: Need to synthesize 38 from known (Z)-vinyl iodide 37.
thought.decision: To achieve this, developed a 5-step synthesis from (Z)-vinyl iodide 37, expecting to obtain 38.

outcome.observation: A 5-step synthesis from the known (Z)-vinyl iodide 37 was developed to obtain 38.

evidence.text_span: No general method to synthesize trisubstituted (Z)-β-silyl acrylates of the type 38 is known in the literature. We thus developed a 5- step synthesis from the known (Z)-vinyl iodide 3727 3727 (see SI for synthetic details).

thought.gap: Need to transform 38 to its corresponding silyl triflate 39 by phenyl protodesilylation.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose TfOH for phenyl protodesilylation, expecting 39.

outcome.observation: 38 could be transformed to its corresponding silyl triflate 39 by phenyl protodesilylation with TfOH.

evidence.text_span: 38 could be transformed to its corresponding silyl triflate 39 by phenyl protodesilylation with TfOH,28 which successfully silylated secondary alcohol 31 to obtain 40 (92%), our new substrate for the intramolecular cycloaddition.

thought.background: Silyl triflate 39 is available, as is secondary alcohol 31.
thought.gap: Need to silylate 31 with 39 to obtain 40, the new substrate for intramolecular cycloaddition.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose silylation of 31 with 39, expecting 40.

outcome.observation: Silyl triflate 39 successfully silylated secondary alcohol 31 to obtain 40 (92%), the new substrate for the intramolecular cycloaddition.

evidence.text_span: 38 could be transformed to its corresponding silyl triflate 39 by phenyl protodesilylation with TfOH,28 which successfully silylated secondary alcohol 31 to obtain 40 (92%), our new substrate for the intramolecular cycloaddition.

thought.gap: Need to perform the intramolecular [2+2] cycloaddition to form 41.
thought.decision: To achieve this, screened several photocycloaddition conditions with 40, expecting the desired product 41.

outcome.observation: Upon screening several photocycloaddition conditions with trisubstituted acrylate 40, no desired product 41 was obtained.

evidence.text_span: Disappointingly, upon screening several photocycloaddition conditions with trisubstituted acrylate 40, we again did not obtain any desired product 41.

thought.background: The intramolecular [2+2] cycloaddition with trisubstituted acrylate 40 failed, possibly due to steric hindrance.
thought.gap: Need to prepare a less hindered alkene that could facilitate C−C bond formation in this congested setting.
thought.decision: To overcome this, decided to prepare 1,2-disubstituted acrylate 42, which would necessitate installation of the quaternary C-15 methyl via alkylation of 43 post cycloaddition. This involves coupling alcohol 31 with known (E)-β-silyl acrylate 45.

evidence.text_span: Reasoning that steric hindrance might be a factor in the failed [2 + 2] cycloaddition, we focused on the preparation of a less hindered alkene that could facilitate C−C bond formation in this congested setting (Scheme 3). Specifically, we sought to prepare 1,2-disubstituted acrylate 42, which would necessitate installation of the quaternary C-15 methyl via alkylation of 43 post cycloaddition. For this purpose, the known (E)-β-silyl acrylate 4529 4529 was coupled with alcohol 31 under the same conditions as above to give silyl ether 46 in 93% yield.

thought.background: Alcohol 31 is available, as is known (E)-β-silyl acrylate 45.
thought.gap: Need to couple 31 with 45 to give silyl ether 46, the less hindered substrate for the intramolecular cycloaddition.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose to couple 31 with 45 under the same silylation conditions as before (TfOH, pyridine), expecting 46.

outcome.observation: The known (E)-β-silyl acrylate 45 was coupled with alcohol 31 under the same conditions as above to give silyl ether 46 in 93% yield.

evidence.text_span: For this purpose, the known (E)-β-silyl acrylate 4529 4529 was coupled with alcohol 31 under the same conditions as above to give silyl ether 46 in 93% yield.

thought.gap: Need to perform the intramolecular [2+2] cycloaddition to form key cyclobutane 47.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose irradiation at 370 nm, expecting efficient cycloaddition.

outcome.observation: Disubstituted acrylate 46 proved to be an excellent substrate for the desired [2+2] cycloaddition, delivering key cyclobutane 47 in 89% yield upon irradiation at 370 nm. The regiochemical outcome was confirmed by 2D NMR analysis.

evidence.text_span: In contrast to the prior Si-tethered acrylate 40, disubstituted acrylate 46 proved to be an excellent substrate for the desired [2 + 2] cycloaddition, delivering key cyclobutane 47 in 89% yield upon irradiation at 370 nm .30 The regiochemical outcome of this step was confirmed by 2D NMR analysis and had been expected based on ring-size considerations.22

thought.gap: Need to convert its C-11 ketone into the required disubstituted alkene via enol triflate formation.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose KHMDS/PhNTf2 for enol triflate formation, expecting 48.

outcome.observation: Its C-11 ketone was converted into the required disubstituted alkene via formation of enol triflate 48 (KHMDS/PhNTf2, 76%).

evidence.text_span: Its C-11 ketone was converted into the required disubstituted alkene via formation of enol triflate 48 (KHMDS/PhNTf2, 76%)31 followed by Pd-catalyzed reduction with formic acid to furnish 49 (92%).32

thought.gap: Need to perform Pd-catalyzed reduction to furnish 49.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose Pd(OAc)2, PPh3, and formic acid with Bu3N, expecting 49.

outcome.observation: Pd-catalyzed reduction with formic acid furnished 49 (92%).

evidence.text_span: Its C-11 ketone was converted into the required disubstituted alkene via formation of enol triflate 48 (KHMDS/PhNTf2, 76%)31 followed by Pd-catalyzed reduction with formic acid to furnish 49 (92%).32

thought.gap: Need to excise the bridging dimethylsilyl group to provide alcohol 50.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose TBAF·3H2O for protodesilylation, expecting 50.

outcome.observation: The bridging dimethylsilyl group was excised using TBAF·3H2O to provide alcohol 50 (94%).

evidence.text_span: We subsequently excised the bridging dimethylsilyl group using TBAF 3H2O to provide alcohol 50 (94%)).33

thought.gap: Need to chemoselectively reduce the lactam in 50 to amine 51.
thought.decision: To achieve this, chose treatment with Vaska’s catalyst and (Me2SiH)2O (TMDS), followed by addition of NaBH3CN to the same pot, expecting amine 51.

outcome.observation: The lactam in 50 was chemoselectively reduced to amine 51 via treatment with Vaska’s catalyst and (Me2SiH)2O (TMDS), followed by addition of NaBH3CN to the same pot (76%).

evidence.text_span: The lactam in 50 could then be chemoselectively reduced to amine 51 via treatment with Vaska’s catalyst and (Me2SiH)2O (TMDS), followed by addition of NaBH3CN to the same pot (76%).34

thought.background: Amine 51 is available. The goal is to construct the C-15 quaternary stereocenter via methylation of the enolate of 51.
thought.gap: Need to perform enolate methylation on TMS ether 52.
thought.decision: To achieve this, attempted enolate methylation on TMS ether 52, expecting productive methylation.

outcome.observation: Initial enolate methylation experiments on TMS ether 52 gave no productive methylation, with unreacted starting material returned.

evidence.text_span: Indeed, initial enolate methylation experiments on TMS ether 52 gave no productive methylation, with unreacted starting material returned; in contrast, free alcohol 51 showed improved reactivity.

thought.background: Amine 51 is available. TMS ether 52 failed to methylate. Free alcohol 51 showed improved reactivity.
thought.gap: Need to construct the C-15 quaternary stereocenter via methylation of the enolate of 51 to give alkylation product 55 (or its C-15 epimer).
thought.decision: To achieve this, treated 51 with >2 equiv of strong base followed by addition of a methyl electrophile, expecting alkylation product 55.

outcome.observation: Surprisingly, treatment with >2 equiv of strong base followed by addition of a methyl electrophile did not give the expected alkylation product 55 (or its C-15 epimer); instead, annotinolide B (3) was serendipitously produced, implying lactonization of intermediate alkoxide 54 following methylation.

evidence.text_span: Surprisingly, treatment with >2 equiv of strong base followed by addition of a methyl electrophile did not give the expected alkylation product 55 (or its C-15 epimer); instead, annotinolide B (3) was serendipitously produced, implying lactonization of intermediate alkoxide 54 following methylation.

thought.background: Amine 51 is available. Previous attempts to methylate the enolate of 51 directly yielded annotinolide B (3) serendipitously.
thought.gap: Need to optimize the one-pot methylation/lactonization sequence to directly deliver annotinolide B (3).
thought.decision: To achieve this, optimized the reaction conditions, finding that LDA as base was crucial and TMEDA improved the yield significantly, expecting annotinolide B (3).

outcome.observation: Extensive optimization revealed that the choice of LDA as base was crucial for the success of the reaction, and the additive TMEDA improved the yield significantly, delivering 3 in 59% yield. To date, >60 mg of 3 has been prepared.

evidence.text_span: Extensive optimization (see SI for full details) revealed that the choice of LDA as base was crucial for the success of the reaction, and the additive TMEDA improved the yield significantly, delivering 3 in 59% yield. To date, >60 mg of 3 has been prepared.

validation.significance: This is the first total synthesis of the complex cyclobutane-containing Lycopodium alkaloid annotinolide B. The approach leverages a novel sequential quinoline dearomatization strategy and highlights the importance of a specific (E)-β-silyl acrylate for the success of the key [2+2] photocycloaddition.

source.title: Total Synthesis of Annotinolide B via Sequential Quinoline Dearomatization.
